romania.title: Centrul Cultural Clujean este organizația gazdă a centrului EUROPE DIRECT Cluj pentru mandatul 2026-2030
romania.summary: Prin găzduirea EUROPE DIRECT Cluj, ne extindem rolul de platformă de dialog civic și cultural, conectând comunitatea locală la temele europene. Pentru CCC, această conexiune este firească, prin cultură, creăm… Articolul Centrul Cultural Clujean este organizația gazdă a centrului EUROPE DIRECT Cluj pentru mandatul 2026-2030 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Atelier de explorare foto în natura sălbatică a Bucureștiului — cu fotograful Alex Busuioceanu
romania.summary: Format ca muzician clasic, Alex Busuioceanu a evadat din rigoarea metronomului prin vizorul aparatului de fotografiat. Încă de acum 18 ani, fotografia a devenit meseria lui – un drum care… Articolul Atelier de explorare foto în natura sălbatică a Bucureștiului — cu fotograful Alex Busuioceanu apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Consiliul Suprem de Apărare a Ţării tocmai a comis ceva care ar putea echivala cu trădarea în ochii multor români! Potrivit unor documente strict secrete, ţara noastră ar urma să interzică grătarele în acest weekend pentru a le permite avioanelor americane să vadă pe unde zboară. „E strigător la cer! Nu s-au atins de grătarul […] The post CSAT: Grătarele vor fi interzise în acest weekend, ca să vadă avioanele americane pe unde merg appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Cântă greierii sub articolele TNR pe Facebook, iar TikTokul arată ca o clădire de birouri din Pipera într-o dimineaţă de duminică! Lipsa misterioasă a roiurilor de troli care cer turul doi ânapoi se explică, însă, foarte simplu: potrivit mai multor surse, Kremlinul a tăiat internetul de mai bine de o zi în cele mai mari […] The post Fenomen rar! romania.summary: Nicușor Dan pare că a pierdut complet contactul cu realitatea. Dintre toate declarațiile eronate pe care le-ar fi putut face, a făcut-o tocmai pe cea mai ruptă de realitate: a pretins – nici mai mai mult, nici mai puțin – că știe să bea de 100 de ori mai bine decât reputatul drojdier nea Costel. romania.title: Genuin, afacere de familie cu așternuturi sustenabile, se extinde în Europa
romania.summary: Genuin, brandul românesc specializat în produse home & deco realizate exclusiv din in 100% european, anunță accelerarea planurilor de extindere internațională, cu sprijinul Băncii Europene pentru...

romania.title: Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov
romania.summary: Joi, 12 februarie, de la ora 18:00, la Librăria Libris (Str. Șt. O. Iosif) din Brașov, Rodica Bretin lansează volumul „Pisica de la ora cinci”, apărut în colecția Corint Fiction. … Read More The post Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

medical.title: Dynamic gel helps scientists grow organs more reliably in the lab
medical.summary: Miniature organs grown in the lab can organize themselves into complex shapes. But they never do it the same way twice, which makes it hard to use these so-called "organoids" to study disease. Now, scientists at UC San Francisco have created a new material that helps organoids grow in a more predictable way. They mixed microparticles of alginate, a complex carbohydrate derived from algae, into Matrigel, the standard gel used to grow organoids. This made the gel more akin to the soft but supportive environment inside the body that tissues normally grow in.

medical.title: Meet the OAPs (old age protesters) getting arrested for climate activism
medical.summary: The boomers spending their golden years getting nicked in the name of ‘intergenerational justice’ The post Meet the OAPs (old age protesters) getting arrested for climate activism appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Disposable vapes ban could lead young adults to switch to cigarettes, study finds
medical.summary: The disposable vapes ban in the UK could lead to young adults switching to alternative products, including cigarettes, new research led by the University of Bristol has found.

medical.title: Why multitasking still fails: Study shows brain can't fully do two tasks at once, even with extensive practice
medical.summary: Even with highly extensive training, the human brain is not really capable of performing two tasks simultaneously. Moreover, even the smallest deviations from trained routines can have a significant impact on how quickly and successfully people complete tasks simultaneously.

medical.title: A 3D printable scaffold to support fast bone growth
medical.summary: A bone-like composite developed at EPFL uses naturally occurring enzymes to accelerate mineralization through an energy-efficient, room-temperature process. The strong, lightweight material shows promise for bone repair applications.

medical.title: Could a hot cup of matcha dial down the 'sneeze switch' in allergic rhinitis?
medical.summary: There's now another reason to love Japan's famous matcha: A study in mice suggests that the green tea powder could reduce the need to sneeze in people with nasal allergies.

medical.title: Study links higher asprosin to less weight gain after menopause
medical.summary: Researchers at the University of California, Irvine Joe C. Wen School of Population & Public Health have identified how the hormone asprosin influences long-term weight change among postmenopausal women in the United States. The findings suggest that the fasting-induced hormone may play a significant role in shaping body composition and long-term weight stability, offering a promising target for tailored obesity prevention strategies.

medical.title: Cellular changes linked to depression related fatigue
medical.summary: Researchers may have discovered a new way to diagnose and treat major depression at the earliest stage of the condition, giving patients the best opportunity for recovery. The research is published in Translational Psychiatry.

medical.title: Scientists may have found a pill for sleep apnea
medical.summary: A European clinical trial found that the drug sulthiame significantly reduced breathing interruptions in people with moderate to severe sleep apnea. Patients taking higher doses experienced up to 47% fewer pauses in breathing and improved oxygen levels during sleep. The drug helps stabilize breathing signals in the brain, reducing airway collapse. Scientists say the findings could pave the way for a pill-based alternative to CPAP machines.

medical.title: Blood marker for Alzheimer's may also be useful in heart and kidney diseases
medical.summary: A certain blood protein regarded as an early indicator of Alzheimer's disease also appears to play a role in other disorders. Researchers at DZNE and the Hertie Institute for Clinical Brain Research (HIH) at the University of Tübingen have found that elevated levels of phosphorylated tau protein (pTau) also occur in two lesser-known conditions that primarily affect the heart and kidneys.

medical.title: Spinal stimulation above and below injury restores leg movement and sensory feedback in clinical trial
medical.summary: The effects of spinal cord injuries are complex and multifaceted. People lose not only the ability to control the movement of their limbs, but also the ability to receive sensory feedback from them. Both are critical to generate the coordinated movement involved in walking.

medical.title: Antibiotics can affect the gut microbiome for several years, study shows
medical.summary: Antibiotic treatments can affect the composition of the community of bacteria living in the gut, known as the gut microbiome, for a long time. A new study shows that certain types of antibiotics can be linked to changes in the gut microbiome as long as four to eight years after treatment. The findings have been published in Nature Medicine.

medical.title: Cannabis study finds THC can create false memories
medical.summary: THC doesn’t just blur memories—it can create new ones that never happened. In a controlled experiment, cannabis users were much more likely to recall words that were never shown and struggled with tasks like remembering to do something later. Researchers found that THC disrupted many different memory systems at once. Surprisingly, moderate doses caused memory problems similar to higher doses.

medical.title: Younger stroke survivors face greater concentration, mental health challenges—especially those not employed
medical.summary: Analysis of a large, nationally representative survey shows that stroke survivors under age 50 have more problems concentrating and running errands and experience more poor mental health days than older stroke survivors do. Younger survivors who were not working faced the greatest challenges in their recovery. The study comes as stroke rates among younger people have increased rapidly in recent years, driven in part by sedentary lifestyles and rising obesity rates.

medical.title: ADHD gender gap tied to delayed diagnosis and poor outcomes
medical.summary: Females diagnosed with ADHD later in life are more likely to experience adolescent mental health struggles, teenage pregnancy, secondary school absences, and have more hospital appointments than those diagnosed in early childhood, finds new research. ADHD, a common neurodevelopmental condition, is less likely to be diagnosed in females than males. Females are also more likely than males to be diagnosed later on in life.

medical.title: Maternal physical activity linked to child neurodevelopment
medical.summary: Higher maternal physical activity is associated with early child neurodevelopment, according to a study published online March 3 in JAMA Network Open.

medical.title: Interviews with 14 recovered adults map common steps out of long-term fatigue
medical.summary: For people who have recovered from diagnoses characterized by persistent fatigue, a new understanding of symptoms seems to have been key to recovery. This is the conclusion of a study from Linköping University, Sweden. The researchers have identified common steps and turning points in patients' stories, which can contribute to new ways of understanding and rehabilitating these conditions. The paper is published in the Scandinavian Journal of Primary Health Care.

medical.title: Making sense of the widening gender mental health gap: What teenage girls told us
medical.summary: Women experience greater low mood and anxiety than men. This longstanding gender mental health gap reflects a complicated mix of biological, psychological, social, and sociocultural factors—though we are still far from fully understanding it.

medical.title: Targeted treatments plus engineered immune cells may slow early spread of triple‑negative breast cancer, study reveals
medical.summary: A new study has revealed a promising new approach to curb the spread of triple-negative breast cancer, one of the most aggressive and difficult-to-treat forms of the disease. Published recently in Cancer Letters, Gabriel Duda, Ph.D., scientific director of transplant oncology and therapeutics at Houston Methodist Research Institute, and his research team discovered pairing targeted treatments with CAR T-cell therapy may help control cancer recurrence when intervention options are otherwise limited.

medical.title: Drop in daytime activity patterns precedes disability worsening in multiple sclerosis
medical.summary: For people with multiple sclerosis (PwMS), within-person reductions in daytime activity patterns precede clinical disability worsening, according to a study published online March 4 in Neurology.

medical.title: Online spaces can provide bodily comfort, relief and inclusion for some autistic people, study says
medical.summary: Online spaces can provide comfort, relief and inclusion for some autistic adults, and provide important means for expression and for connecting with others, a new study published in Autism in Adulthood says.

medical.title: Low testosterone levels may be associated with increased risk of prostate cancer progression during surveillance
medical.summary: A new study led by researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center found that prostate cancer patients with low testosterone levels may have a higher risk of cancer progressing to a more aggressive form while under active surveillance. The findings, published in the Journal of Urology, suggest that baseline testosterone may serve as a useful clinical marker to better stratify risk and tailor monitoring strategies for patients choosing active surveillance.

medical.title: Adult ADHD trial shows clinically relevant reduction in symptoms
medical.summary: A randomized controlled trial (RCT) published in Psychological Medicine demonstrates a significant and clinically relevant reduction in ADHD symptoms in adults using the digital therapy "attexis."

medical.title: Bad gut bacteria could raise risk of premature death for people with heart failure
medical.summary: Poor gut health could increase the risk of dying early or being hospitalized by almost 10% for people with heart failure, and in the first year after being admitted to hospital with heart failure, people are 8% more likely to die or be readmitted if they show signs of an unhealthy gut, according to a study led by researchers at the University of Leicester published in the European Journal of Preventive Cardiology.

medical.title: LeaN On: Reducing risk of lymphedema after breast cancer
medical.summary: Living with, or being at risk of, lymphedema after breast cancer can leave many people feeling uncertain and overwhelmed. Too often, survivors must search for information on their own, sometimes too late, and without clear guidance on what matters most for their long-term health. To help address this challenge, researchers from Flinders University have developed Lymphedema Navigation Online (LeaN On), a new evidence-based digital platform supporting breast cancer survivors across Australia who are living with, or worried about, lymphedema.

science.title: Ancient Filipino skeleton reveals a rare hip condition further complicated by scurvy
science.summary: The growing paleopathological literature shows that scurvy was not a rare problem among people living in the ancient Asia-Pacific tropics. Scurvy is increasingly identified throughout the region, primarily in children but also in adults. In a study published in the International Journal of Osteoarchaeology, Dr. Chloe Boucher and her colleagues conducted a follow-up paleopathological study of a young adult male from the Philippines' Metal Period (~2000–1800 BP), previously identified as having suffered from hip ankylosis.

science.title: Study captures single polymer segments sticking and slipping on surfaces
science.summary: Kyushu University researchers have directly observed, for the first time, how individual polymers—chain-like molecules—behave when in contact with solid surfaces. Published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, the study reveals a previously unseen behavior in which molecules repeatedly stick to and release from the surface. The findings may contribute to enhancing the performance of adhesives for joining different materials.

science.title: Flash heating upcycles waste glass into SiC nanowires in seconds
science.summary: Engineering silicon carbide (SiC) with tailored morphologies for electronics and structural reinforcement materials has always been a costly and time-consuming affair, but scientists can now do it in a flash. A new study shows how discarded glass and silicon-rich coal waste can be turned into valuable SiC nanowires in seconds using a process known as Fluorine-Assisted Flash (FAF) Joule heating, where a quick pulse of electricity instantly heats up the reaction mixture to extremely high temperatures.

science.title: Researchers mix X-rays and optical light to track speedy electrons in materials
science.summary: To unlock materials of the future, including better photocatalysts or light-switchable superconductors, researchers need to understand how the valence electrons within materials respond to light at the atomic scale. Materials are made of atoms, and an atom's outer electrons, or valence electrons, are responsible for chemical bonding as well as a material's thermal, magnetic, and electronic properties.

science.title: Hydrogen atmosphere could keep exomoons habitable for billions of years
science.summary: Liquid water is considered essential for life. Surprisingly, however, stable conditions that are conducive to life could exist far from any sun. A research team from the Excellence Cluster ORIGINS at LMU and the Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics (MPE) has shown that moons around free-floating planets can keep their water oceans liquid for up to 4.3 billion years by virtue of dense hydrogen atmospheres and tidal heating—that is to say, for almost as long as Earth has existed and sufficient time for complex life to develop.

science.title: Scalable quantum batteries can charge faster than their classical counterparts
science.summary: Over the past decades, energy engineers have developed increasingly advanced battery technologies that can store more energy, charge faster and maintain their performance for longer. In recent years, some researchers have also started exploring the potential of quantum batteries, devices that can store energy leveraging quantum mechanical effects.

science.title: Scientists discover tiny plant trick that could supercharge crop yields
science.summary: Researchers have uncovered a molecular trick used by hornwort plants that could help future crops capture carbon dioxide more efficiently. A unique protein feature called RbcS-STAR causes the key photosynthesis enzyme Rubisco to cluster into dense compartments, helping it work more effectively. When scientists added this feature to other plants, Rubisco reorganized in the same way. The finding raises the possibility of engineering more efficient photosynthesis into major crops.

science.title: DNA barcoding reveals which gene-therapy nanoparticles reach targets in vivo
science.summary: Drug delivery researchers have vastly improved the potential of genetic therapies by overcoming the challenge of consistently getting genes and gene-editing tools where they need to be within cells. Findings of the study spearheaded by Oregon State University College of Pharmacy graduate student Antony Jozić are published in Nature Biotechnology.

science.title: Watching quantum behavior in action: MagnetoARPES reveals time-reversal symmetry breaking in a kagome superconductor
science.summary: Electron movement and structures described in quantum physics allow researchers to better understand how and why materials like superconductors behave as they do. Rice University researchers Jianwei Huang and Ming Yi have developed a new capability, magnetoARPES, building on angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) that allows researchers to study quantum behaviors they have been unable to resolve using ARPES alone. The work has been published in Nature Physics.

science.title: New research shows path to affordable water in fast-growing cities
science.summary: By 2050, up to half the world's urban population will face water scarcity. A new model of water supply, demand, and policies in a drought-prone city of 7 million in India shows how policies could prevent the poor from bearing the heaviest burden.

science.title: Oval orbit casts new light on black hole–neutron star mergers
science.summary: Scientists have uncovered the first robust evidence of a black hole and neutron star crashing together but orbiting in an oval path rather than a perfect circle just before they merged. This discovery challenges long-standing assumptions about how these cosmic pairs form and evolve.

science.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4825-4831: Exploring the Borderlands
science.summary: Written by William Farrand, Senior Research Scientist, Space Science Institute Earth planning date: Friday, March 6, 2026 Curiosity is in the last stage of its exploration of the spiderweb-like boxwork unit. This stage consists of exploring the eastern and southern borders of this terrain. There were two multi-sol plans assembled this week. The previous plan […]

science.title: A Most Unusual Lake
science.summary: Lake Unter-See in Antarctica, sealed beneath thick ice, contains unusually high levels of dissolved oxygen and cone-shaped microbial reefs resembling some of Earth’s oldest fossils.

science.title: A particle accelerator helped scientists create stunning 3D ants
science.summary: Researchers have developed a high-tech system that rapidly scans ants and converts them into detailed 3D models. Using a synchrotron accelerator, X-ray imaging, robotics, and AI, the team scanned 2,000 specimens in just a week and produced models of 800 species. The images reveal microscopic anatomy that was previously difficult to study. The growing Antscan database could become a powerful digital library of biodiversity.

science.title: About Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools Project
science.summary: Project Overview NASA’s Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT) project develops technologies and tools for various types of aircraft that fly in different speed regimes, including next-generation vertical take-off and landing and fixed-wing subsonic aircraft. The research advances knowledge, technologies, and concepts that enable major steps to lowering operating costs of the next-generation single-aisle aircraft. […]

science.title: Vegans develop complex skills to navigate an omnivorous society, new research shows
science.summary: Going vegan is a life-changing decision. Successfully committing to eating only ethically sourced, non-exploitative products—no dairy, no honey, no eggs, no animal output of any kind—can be daunting, especially in a society where most people are omnivorous. Foregoing meat and other animal products purely for ethical reasons can cause tension between vegans and their friends, families, partners, businesses and even other vegans.

science.title: The 19th-century mathematical clue that led to quantum mechanics
science.summary: More than a century before quantum mechanics was born, Irish mathematician William Rowan Hamilton stumbled onto an idea that would quietly foreshadow one of the deepest truths in physics. While studying the paths of light rays and moving objects, Hamilton noticed a striking mathematical similarity between them and used it to develop a powerful new framework for mechanics. At the time, it seemed like a clever analogy—but decades later, as scientists uncovered the strange wave-particle nature of light and matter, Hamilton’s insight took on new meaning.

science.title: Convergence in the canopy: Why the Gracixalus weii treefrog sounds like a songbird
science.summary: The genus Gracixalus belongs to the family of Old World Tree Frogs and is geographically dispersed from Myanmar and western Thailand to Laos, Vietnam, and further to southern China. Despite the considerable amount of research on the species richness of Gracixalus, little is known about their vocalizations.

science.title: Scientists turn scrap car aluminum into high-performance metal for new vehicles
science.summary: Scientists at Oak Ridge National Laboratory have created a new aluminum alloy called RidgeAlloy that can turn contaminated car-body scrap into strong structural vehicle parts. Normally, impurities introduced during recycling make this scrap unsuitable for high-performance applications. RidgeAlloy overcomes that challenge, enabling recycled aluminum to meet the strength and durability standards required for modern vehicles. The technology could slash energy use, reduce imports, and unlock a huge new supply of domestic aluminum.

science.title: How jagged moon dust could support future astronauts
science.summary: Lunar dust can be a pain—but it's also literally the ground we will have to traverse if we are ever to have a permanent human settlement on the moon. In that specific use case, its clingy, jagged, staticky properties can actually be an advantage, according to a new paper recently published in Research from scientists at Beihang University, who analyzed the mechanical properties of samples returned by the Chang'e 6 mission to the far side of the moon.

science.title: Foraged mushrooms and sea beet featured in British meals in the 16th century. Why not today?
science.summary: Wild garlic, oyster mushrooms and sea beet were once regularly gathered and eaten as part of meals across the UK. Today, some people have concerns about eating food growing in the woods or hedgerows, but are keen to discuss why—as our research shows.

science.title: Hybrid synthetic strategy unlocks previously unattainable molecular architectures
science.summary: The molecular-scale design of materials is one of the major frontiers in modern science. Flat, highly conjugated organic molecules are already used in advanced technologies such as chemical sensors, optoelectronic devices, and energy conversion systems. One of the most promising strategies to enhance their performance involves "linking" multiple units together, extending their electronic structure and thereby modifying their properties.

science.title: New African species confirms evolutionary origin of magic mushrooms
science.summary: A long-standing debate about the evolutionary origin of the world's most widely cultivated "magic mushroom"—Psilocybe cubensis—may now have been settled by scientists from southern Africa and the United States.

science.title: Scientists document Europe's first Jurassic lizard trackways in Asturias
science.summary: The article presenting the research results was published online at the end of February in the journal Ichnos. The study focuses on two trackways (T1 and T2) preserved as convex hyporeliefs on the underside of a Late Jurassic sandstone layer, approximately 152 million years old, from the cliffs east of Playa de España (Villaviciosa). The specimens are now part of the museum's collection and are on display in the gallery dedicated to the Asturian Jurassic.

science.title: Language difficulties can hinder young children's social autonomy
science.summary: When considering the challenges faced by children with developmental language disorder (DLD), it's natural to think of difficulties they have in understanding and using language. What tends to get overlooked, however, is how the disorder impacts how their functioning in society.

environment.title: Local conservation projects restore habitats for wildlife
environment.summary: Community-led restoration efforts are improving biodiversity in several regions.

environment.title: Middle East conflict exposes Africa’s fossil fuel risks & the case for clean energy
environment.summary: A deepening crisis in the Middle East could send economic shockwaves across sub-Saharan Africa, raising fuel costs, food prices and inflation across the region, according to a new analysis by energy consultancy Zero Carbon Analytics. Roughly one-fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas flows through the Strait of Hormuz between Iran, Oman and […]

environment.title: Can Singapore rewild its lost reptiles?
environment.summary: In the older quarters of Singapore, fragments of forest persist like memories that refuse to fade. Within them live creatures that few residents ever see, and some that no longer exist there at all. The disappearance of species on an island is not always dramatic. Often it happens quietly, in decades of thinning habitat and […]

environment.title: Cambodian market survey a snapshot of a resilient — but stressed — Mekong
environment.summary: STUNG TRENG, Cambodia — Sunrise is still a long way off when the first fishing boats slip into the landing site at this provincial town along the Mekong River in northern Cambodia. The night’s catch is hauled ashore and moved in tubs and woven baskets to a nearby side street off the town’s main boulevard. […]

environment.title: Indigenous knowledge helps guide conservation of Australia’s endangered northern quoll
environment.summary: Despite its widespread biodiversity, Australia holds an unenviable record when it comes to wildlife: the highest mammalian extinction rate in the world. Since the arrival of Europeans and colonial expansion, at least 40 terrestrial mammal species have been lost, and others are facing serious threats. Notable among these is the northern quoll (Dasyurus hallucatus), a […]

environment.title: Ecuador’s new ecological corridor connects Andes and Amazon ecosystems
environment.summary: Ecuador has announced the creation of a new biological corridor designed to connect the eastern ranges of the Andes with the Amazon Rainforest, part of a larger initiative to strengthen ecological connectivity and protect biodiversity. The Llanganates–Yasuní Connectivity Corridor, officially announced this month, spans 2,159 square kilometers (834 square miles) across two provinces, connecting Llanganates […]

environment.title: In Malawi, farmers rebuild soil and livelihoods through agroecology
environment.summary: MZIMBA, Malawi – For years, life was defined by hardship for Grena Banda and her husband, Daniel Mwafulirwa, in Malawi’s northern district of Rumphi. Their small farm was their only reliable source of livelihood, yet it rarely produced enough. Climate change brought erratic rainfall, sometimes drought and sometimes heavy downpours that washed away fragile topsoil. […]

environment.title: David Chivers, student of the singing apes
environment.summary: Field primatology expanded rapidly in the late 20th century as biologists began to study apes and monkeys where they lived rather than only in museums or laboratories. Southeast Asia’s rainforests became an important setting for that shift. Among the researchers who helped shape the discipline there was David Chivers, a British primatologist whose work […]

environment.title: Critically endangered kākāpō parrot has standout breeding season
environment.summary: A total of 59 healthy kākāpō chicks have hatched over the last few weeks, according to the latest tally by Aotearoa New Zealand’s Department of Conservation. This marks one of the most successful recent breeding seasons for this critically endangered bird, whose last breeding season was four years ago. The kākāpō (Strigops habroptilus), a flightless bird […]

environment.title: Ugandans affected by pipeline discontented over rehabilitation efforts: Report
environment.summary: People whose land was acquired by the Ugandan government for the construction of the East African Crude Oil Pipeline (EACOP) say livelihood restoration programs offered by project developers have not changed their lives for the better, a new report says. The 1,443-kilometer (897-mile) crude oil pipeline — the longest heated crude oil pipeline in the […]

environment.title: Thailand tightens embrace of fossil fuels amid Middle East conflict
environment.summary: On March 4, Thailand’s government ordered the Ministry of Energy to secure new energy sources within a week to reduce the nation’s reliance on Middle Eastern oil. The directive follows the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, between the Persian Gulf and the Arabian Sea, after the Feb. 28 bombing of Iran by the U.S. […]

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Precision conservation: the rise of place-specific strategies where protection works best
environment.summary: Conservation has long wrestled with a deceptively simple question: not whether to act, but where action will matter most. Forest restoration, protected areas, wildlife corridors, and enforcement patrols all compete for limited funding across landscapes that differ enormously in ecology, governance, and human pressures. A growing body of research argues that improving outcomes depends less […]

environment.title: Restoration of Brazil’s Atlantic Forest may hinge on market for native plants
environment.summary: The forests of the world are teeming with life, from the towering trees down to the microscopic organisms that quietly recycle and refuel the soil. That’s why clearing forests leads to biodiversity loss, problems with water and soil quality, and less carbon storage. In Brazil’s Atlantic Forest, restoration projects have been underway for decades to […]

environment.title: Indonesian communities push to reclaim lands following company permit revocations
environment.summary: MEDAN, Indonesia — Sahala Pasaribu, 35, walks on customary land his family has not been able to manage for more than three decades since PT Toba Pulp Lestari (TPL), a pulpwood company, took control of it. Now, after the government decided to revoke the company’s permit in January, he hopes it’s possible. “We feel free […]

mix_items.summary: Nicușor Dan pare că a pierdut complet contactul cu realitatea. Dintre toate declarațiile eronate pe care le-ar fi putut face, a făcut-o tocmai pe cea mai ruptă de realitate: a pretins – nici mai mai mult, nici mai puțin – că știe să bea de 100 de ori mai bine decât reputatul drojdier nea Costel. […] The post A luat-o razna! Nicușor i-a spus lui nea Costel că știe să bea de 100 de ori mai bine decât el appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Prin găzduirea EUROPE DIRECT Cluj, ne extindem rolul de platformă de dialog civic și cultural, conectând comunitatea locală la temele europene. Pentru CCC, această conexiune este firească, prin cultură, creăm… Articolul Centrul Cultural Clujean este organizația gazdă a centrului EUROPE DIRECT Cluj pentru mandatul 2026-2030 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Atelier de explorare foto în natura sălbatică a Bucureștiului — cu fotograful Alex Busuioceanu
mix_items.summary: Format ca muzician clasic, Alex Busuioceanu a evadat din rigoarea metronomului prin vizorul aparatului de fotografiat. Încă de acum 18 ani, fotografia a devenit meseria lui – un drum care… Articolul Atelier de explorare foto în natura sălbatică a Bucureștiului — cu fotograful Alex Busuioceanu apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Genuin, afacere de familie cu așternuturi sustenabile, se extinde în Europa
mix_items.summary: Genuin, brandul românesc specializat în produse home & deco realizate exclusiv din in 100% european, anunță accelerarea planurilor de extindere internațională, cu sprijinul Băncii Europene pentru...

mix_items.title: 😂 Fenomen rar! Zero comentarii cu „Turu doi ânapoi” de când s-a tăiat internetul în Rusia
mix_items.summary: Cântă greierii sub articolele TNR pe Facebook, iar TikTokul arată ca o clădire de birouri din Pipera într-o dimineaţă de duminică! Lipsa misterioasă a roiurilor de troli care cer turul doi ânapoi se explică, însă, foarte simplu: potrivit mai multor surse, Kremlinul a tăiat internetul de mai bine de o zi în cele mai mari […] The post Fenomen rar! Zero comentarii cu „Turu doi ânapoi” de când s-a tăiat internetul în Rusia appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov
mix_items.summary: Joi, 12 februarie, de la ora 18:00, la Librăria Libris (Str. Șt. O. Iosif) din Brașov, Rodica Bretin lansează volumul „Pisica de la ora cinci”, apărut în colecția Corint Fiction. … Read More The post Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Dynamic gel helps scientists grow organs more reliably in the lab
mix_items.summary: Miniature organs grown in the lab can organize themselves into complex shapes. But they never do it the same way twice, which makes it hard to use these so-called "organoids" to study disease. Now, scientists at UC San Francisco have created a new material that helps organoids grow in a more predictable way. They mixed microparticles of alginate, a complex carbohydrate derived from algae, into Matrigel, the standard gel used to grow organoids. This made the gel more akin to the soft but supportive environment inside the body that tissues normally grow in.

mix_items.title: 😂 CSAT: Grătarele vor fi interzise în acest weekend, ca să vadă avioanele americane pe unde merg
mix_items.summary: Consiliul Suprem de Apărare a Ţării tocmai a comis ceva care ar putea echivala cu trădarea în ochii multor români! Potrivit unor documente strict secrete, ţara noastră ar urma să interzică grătarele în acest weekend pentru a le permite avioanelor americane să vadă pe unde zboară. „E strigător la cer! Nu s-au atins de grătarul […] The post CSAT: Grătarele vor fi interzise în acest weekend, ca să vadă avioanele americane pe unde merg appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Ancient Filipino skeleton reveals a rare hip condition further complicated by scurvy
mix_items.summary: The growing paleopathological literature shows that scurvy was not a rare problem among people living in the ancient Asia-Pacific tropics. Scurvy is increasingly identified throughout the region, primarily in children but also in adults. In a study published in the International Journal of Osteoarchaeology, Dr. Chloe Boucher and her colleagues conducted a follow-up paleopathological study of a young adult male from the Philippines' Metal Period (~2000–1800 BP), previously identified as having suffered from hip ankylosis.

mix_items.title: Meet the OAPs (old age protesters) getting arrested for climate activism
mix_items.summary: The boomers spending their golden years getting nicked in the name of ‘intergenerational justice’ The post Meet the OAPs (old age protesters) getting arrested for climate activism appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Disposable vapes ban could lead young adults to switch to cigarettes, study finds
mix_items.summary: The disposable vapes ban in the UK could lead to young adults switching to alternative products, including cigarettes, new research led by the University of Bristol has found.

mix_items.title: Why multitasking still fails: Study shows brain can't fully do two tasks at once, even with extensive practice
mix_items.summary: Even with highly extensive training, the human brain is not really capable of performing two tasks simultaneously. Moreover, even the smallest deviations from trained routines can have a significant impact on how quickly and successfully people complete tasks simultaneously.

mix_items.title: Study captures single polymer segments sticking and slipping on surfaces
mix_items.summary: Kyushu University researchers have directly observed, for the first time, how individual polymers—chain-like molecules—behave when in contact with solid surfaces. Published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, the study reveals a previously unseen behavior in which molecules repeatedly stick to and release from the surface. The findings may contribute to enhancing the performance of adhesives for joining different materials.

mix_items.title: Flash heating upcycles waste glass into SiC nanowires in seconds
mix_items.summary: Engineering silicon carbide (SiC) with tailored morphologies for electronics and structural reinforcement materials has always been a costly and time-consuming affair, but scientists can now do it in a flash. A new study shows how discarded glass and silicon-rich coal waste can be turned into valuable SiC nanowires in seconds using a process known as Fluorine-Assisted Flash (FAF) Joule heating, where a quick pulse of electricity instantly heats up the reaction mixture to extremely high temperatures.

mix_items.title: A 3D printable scaffold to support fast bone growth
mix_items.summary: A bone-like composite developed at EPFL uses naturally occurring enzymes to accelerate mineralization through an energy-efficient, room-temperature process. The strong, lightweight material shows promise for bone repair applications.

mix_items.title: Researchers mix X-rays and optical light to track speedy electrons in materials
mix_items.summary: To unlock materials of the future, including better photocatalysts or light-switchable superconductors, researchers need to understand how the valence electrons within materials respond to light at the atomic scale. Materials are made of atoms, and an atom's outer electrons, or valence electrons, are responsible for chemical bonding as well as a material's thermal, magnetic, and electronic properties.

mix_items.title: Could a hot cup of matcha dial down the 'sneeze switch' in allergic rhinitis?
mix_items.summary: There's now another reason to love Japan's famous matcha: A study in mice suggests that the green tea powder could reduce the need to sneeze in people with nasal allergies.

mix_items.title: Hydrogen atmosphere could keep exomoons habitable for billions of years
mix_items.summary: Liquid water is considered essential for life. Surprisingly, however, stable conditions that are conducive to life could exist far from any sun. A research team from the Excellence Cluster ORIGINS at LMU and the Max Planck Institute for Extraterrestrial Physics (MPE) has shown that moons around free-floating planets can keep their water oceans liquid for up to 4.3 billion years by virtue of dense hydrogen atmospheres and tidal heating—that is to say, for almost as long as Earth has existed and sufficient time for complex life to develop.

mix_items.title: Study links higher asprosin to less weight gain after menopause
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of California, Irvine Joe C. Wen School of Population & Public Health have identified how the hormone asprosin influences long-term weight change among postmenopausal women in the United States. The findings suggest that the fasting-induced hormone may play a significant role in shaping body composition and long-term weight stability, offering a promising target for tailored obesity prevention strategies.

mix_items.title: Cellular changes linked to depression related fatigue
mix_items.summary: Researchers may have discovered a new way to diagnose and treat major depression at the earliest stage of the condition, giving patients the best opportunity for recovery. The research is published in Translational Psychiatry.

mix_items.title: Scalable quantum batteries can charge faster than their classical counterparts
mix_items.summary: Over the past decades, energy engineers have developed increasingly advanced battery technologies that can store more energy, charge faster and maintain their performance for longer. In recent years, some researchers have also started exploring the potential of quantum batteries, devices that can store energy leveraging quantum mechanical effects.

mix_items.title: Scientists may have found a pill for sleep apnea
mix_items.summary: A European clinical trial found that the drug sulthiame significantly reduced breathing interruptions in people with moderate to severe sleep apnea. Patients taking higher doses experienced up to 47% fewer pauses in breathing and improved oxygen levels during sleep. The drug helps stabilize breathing signals in the brain, reducing airway collapse. Scientists say the findings could pave the way for a pill-based alternative to CPAP machines.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover tiny plant trick that could supercharge crop yields
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a molecular trick used by hornwort plants that could help future crops capture carbon dioxide more efficiently. A unique protein feature called RbcS-STAR causes the key photosynthesis enzyme Rubisco to cluster into dense compartments, helping it work more effectively. When scientists added this feature to other plants, Rubisco reorganized in the same way. The finding raises the possibility of engineering more efficient photosynthesis into major crops.

mix_items.title: Blood marker for Alzheimer's may also be useful in heart and kidney diseases
mix_items.summary: A certain blood protein regarded as an early indicator of Alzheimer's disease also appears to play a role in other disorders. Researchers at DZNE and the Hertie Institute for Clinical Brain Research (HIH) at the University of Tübingen have found that elevated levels of phosphorylated tau protein (pTau) also occur in two lesser-known conditions that primarily affect the heart and kidneys.

mix_items.title: DNA barcoding reveals which gene-therapy nanoparticles reach targets in vivo
mix_items.summary: Drug delivery researchers have vastly improved the potential of genetic therapies by overcoming the challenge of consistently getting genes and gene-editing tools where they need to be within cells. Findings of the study spearheaded by Oregon State University College of Pharmacy graduate student Antony Jozić are published in Nature Biotechnology.

mix_items.title: Spinal stimulation above and below injury restores leg movement and sensory feedback in clinical trial
mix_items.summary: The effects of spinal cord injuries are complex and multifaceted. People lose not only the ability to control the movement of their limbs, but also the ability to receive sensory feedback from them. Both are critical to generate the coordinated movement involved in walking.

mix_items.title: Watching quantum behavior in action: MagnetoARPES reveals time-reversal symmetry breaking in a kagome superconductor
mix_items.summary: Electron movement and structures described in quantum physics allow researchers to better understand how and why materials like superconductors behave as they do. Rice University researchers Jianwei Huang and Ming Yi have developed a new capability, magnetoARPES, building on angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) that allows researchers to study quantum behaviors they have been unable to resolve using ARPES alone. The work has been published in Nature Physics.

mix_items.title: Antibiotics can affect the gut microbiome for several years, study shows
mix_items.summary: Antibiotic treatments can affect the composition of the community of bacteria living in the gut, known as the gut microbiome, for a long time. A new study shows that certain types of antibiotics can be linked to changes in the gut microbiome as long as four to eight years after treatment. The findings have been published in Nature Medicine.

mix_items.title: New research shows path to affordable water in fast-growing cities
mix_items.summary: By 2050, up to half the world's urban population will face water scarcity. A new model of water supply, demand, and policies in a drought-prone city of 7 million in India shows how policies could prevent the poor from bearing the heaviest burden.

mix_items.title: Oval orbit casts new light on black hole–neutron star mergers
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered the first robust evidence of a black hole and neutron star crashing together but orbiting in an oval path rather than a perfect circle just before they merged. This discovery challenges long-standing assumptions about how these cosmic pairs form and evolve.

mix_items.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4825-4831: Exploring the Borderlands
mix_items.summary: Written by William Farrand, Senior Research Scientist, Space Science Institute Earth planning date: Friday, March 6, 2026 Curiosity is in the last stage of its exploration of the spiderweb-like boxwork unit. This stage consists of exploring the eastern and southern borders of this terrain. There were two multi-sol plans assembled this week. The previous plan […]

mix_items.title: Cannabis study finds THC can create false memories
mix_items.summary: THC doesn’t just blur memories—it can create new ones that never happened. In a controlled experiment, cannabis users were much more likely to recall words that were never shown and struggled with tasks like remembering to do something later. Researchers found that THC disrupted many different memory systems at once. Surprisingly, moderate doses caused memory problems similar to higher doses.

mix_items.title: A Most Unusual Lake
mix_items.summary: Lake Unter-See in Antarctica, sealed beneath thick ice, contains unusually high levels of dissolved oxygen and cone-shaped microbial reefs resembling some of Earth’s oldest fossils.

mix_items.title: A particle accelerator helped scientists create stunning 3D ants
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed a high-tech system that rapidly scans ants and converts them into detailed 3D models. Using a synchrotron accelerator, X-ray imaging, robotics, and AI, the team scanned 2,000 specimens in just a week and produced models of 800 species. The images reveal microscopic anatomy that was previously difficult to study. The growing Antscan database could become a powerful digital library of biodiversity.

mix_items.title: About Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools Project
mix_items.summary: Project Overview NASA’s Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT) project develops technologies and tools for various types of aircraft that fly in different speed regimes, including next-generation vertical take-off and landing and fixed-wing subsonic aircraft. The research advances knowledge, technologies, and concepts that enable major steps to lowering operating costs of the next-generation single-aisle aircraft. […]

mix_items.title: Younger stroke survivors face greater concentration, mental health challenges—especially those not employed
mix_items.summary: Analysis of a large, nationally representative survey shows that stroke survivors under age 50 have more problems concentrating and running errands and experience more poor mental health days than older stroke survivors do. Younger survivors who were not working faced the greatest challenges in their recovery. The study comes as stroke rates among younger people have increased rapidly in recent years, driven in part by sedentary lifestyles and rising obesity rates.

mix_items.title: Vegans develop complex skills to navigate an omnivorous society, new research shows
mix_items.summary: Going vegan is a life-changing decision. Successfully committing to eating only ethically sourced, non-exploitative products—no dairy, no honey, no eggs, no animal output of any kind—can be daunting, especially in a society where most people are omnivorous. Foregoing meat and other animal products purely for ethical reasons can cause tension between vegans and their friends, families, partners, businesses and even other vegans.

mix_items.title: ADHD gender gap tied to delayed diagnosis and poor outcomes
mix_items.summary: Females diagnosed with ADHD later in life are more likely to experience adolescent mental health struggles, teenage pregnancy, secondary school absences, and have more hospital appointments than those diagnosed in early childhood, finds new research. ADHD, a common neurodevelopmental condition, is less likely to be diagnosed in females than males. Females are also more likely than males to be diagnosed later on in life.

mix_items.title: The 19th-century mathematical clue that led to quantum mechanics
mix_items.summary: More than a century before quantum mechanics was born, Irish mathematician William Rowan Hamilton stumbled onto an idea that would quietly foreshadow one of the deepest truths in physics. While studying the paths of light rays and moving objects, Hamilton noticed a striking mathematical similarity between them and used it to develop a powerful new framework for mechanics. At the time, it seemed like a clever analogy—but decades later, as scientists uncovered the strange wave-particle nature of light and matter, Hamilton’s insight took on new meaning.

mix_items.title: Maternal physical activity linked to child neurodevelopment
mix_items.summary: Higher maternal physical activity is associated with early child neurodevelopment, according to a study published online March 3 in JAMA Network Open.

mix_items.title: Interviews with 14 recovered adults map common steps out of long-term fatigue
mix_items.summary: For people who have recovered from diagnoses characterized by persistent fatigue, a new understanding of symptoms seems to have been key to recovery. This is the conclusion of a study from Linköping University, Sweden. The researchers have identified common steps and turning points in patients' stories, which can contribute to new ways of understanding and rehabilitating these conditions. The paper is published in the Scandinavian Journal of Primary Health Care.

mix_items.title: Making sense of the widening gender mental health gap: What teenage girls told us
mix_items.summary: Women experience greater low mood and anxiety than men. This longstanding gender mental health gap reflects a complicated mix of biological, psychological, social, and sociocultural factors—though we are still far from fully understanding it.

mix_items.title: Targeted treatments plus engineered immune cells may slow early spread of triple‑negative breast cancer, study reveals
mix_items.summary: A new study has revealed a promising new approach to curb the spread of triple-negative breast cancer, one of the most aggressive and difficult-to-treat forms of the disease. Published recently in Cancer Letters, Gabriel Duda, Ph.D., scientific director of transplant oncology and therapeutics at Houston Methodist Research Institute, and his research team discovered pairing targeted treatments with CAR T-cell therapy may help control cancer recurrence when intervention options are otherwise limited.

mix_items.title: Convergence in the canopy: Why the Gracixalus weii treefrog sounds like a songbird
mix_items.summary: The genus Gracixalus belongs to the family of Old World Tree Frogs and is geographically dispersed from Myanmar and western Thailand to Laos, Vietnam, and further to southern China. Despite the considerable amount of research on the species richness of Gracixalus, little is known about their vocalizations.

mix_items.title: Scientists turn scrap car aluminum into high-performance metal for new vehicles
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Oak Ridge National Laboratory have created a new aluminum alloy called RidgeAlloy that can turn contaminated car-body scrap into strong structural vehicle parts. Normally, impurities introduced during recycling make this scrap unsuitable for high-performance applications. RidgeAlloy overcomes that challenge, enabling recycled aluminum to meet the strength and durability standards required for modern vehicles. The technology could slash energy use, reduce imports, and unlock a huge new supply of domestic aluminum.

mix_items.title: Drop in daytime activity patterns precedes disability worsening in multiple sclerosis
mix_items.summary: For people with multiple sclerosis (PwMS), within-person reductions in daytime activity patterns precede clinical disability worsening, according to a study published online March 4 in Neurology.

mix_items.title: Online spaces can provide bodily comfort, relief and inclusion for some autistic people, study says
mix_items.summary: Online spaces can provide comfort, relief and inclusion for some autistic adults, and provide important means for expression and for connecting with others, a new study published in Autism in Adulthood says.

mix_items.title: Low testosterone levels may be associated with increased risk of prostate cancer progression during surveillance
mix_items.summary: A new study led by researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center found that prostate cancer patients with low testosterone levels may have a higher risk of cancer progressing to a more aggressive form while under active surveillance. The findings, published in the Journal of Urology, suggest that baseline testosterone may serve as a useful clinical marker to better stratify risk and tailor monitoring strategies for patients choosing active surveillance.

mix_items.title: How jagged moon dust could support future astronauts
mix_items.summary: Lunar dust can be a pain—but it's also literally the ground we will have to traverse if we are ever to have a permanent human settlement on the moon. In that specific use case, its clingy, jagged, staticky properties can actually be an advantage, according to a new paper recently published in Research from scientists at Beihang University, who analyzed the mechanical properties of samples returned by the Chang'e 6 mission to the far side of the moon.

mix_items.title: Adult ADHD trial shows clinically relevant reduction in symptoms
mix_items.summary: A randomized controlled trial (RCT) published in Psychological Medicine demonstrates a significant and clinically relevant reduction in ADHD symptoms in adults using the digital therapy "attexis."

mix_items.title: Bad gut bacteria could raise risk of premature death for people with heart failure
mix_items.summary: Poor gut health could increase the risk of dying early or being hospitalized by almost 10% for people with heart failure, and in the first year after being admitted to hospital with heart failure, people are 8% more likely to die or be readmitted if they show signs of an unhealthy gut, according to a study led by researchers at the University of Leicester published in the European Journal of Preventive Cardiology.

mix_items.title: LeaN On: Reducing risk of lymphedema after breast cancer
mix_items.summary: Living with, or being at risk of, lymphedema after breast cancer can leave many people feeling uncertain and overwhelmed. Too often, survivors must search for information on their own, sometimes too late, and without clear guidance on what matters most for their long-term health. To help address this challenge, researchers from Flinders University have developed Lymphedema Navigation Online (LeaN On), a new evidence-based digital platform supporting breast cancer survivors across Australia who are living with, or worried about, lymphedema.

mix_items.title: Foraged mushrooms and sea beet featured in British meals in the 16th century. Why not today?
mix_items.summary: Wild garlic, oyster mushrooms and sea beet were once regularly gathered and eaten as part of meals across the UK. Today, some people have concerns about eating food growing in the woods or hedgerows, but are keen to discuss why—as our research shows.

mix_items.title: Hybrid synthetic strategy unlocks previously unattainable molecular architectures
mix_items.summary: The molecular-scale design of materials is one of the major frontiers in modern science. Flat, highly conjugated organic molecules are already used in advanced technologies such as chemical sensors, optoelectronic devices, and energy conversion systems. One of the most promising strategies to enhance their performance involves "linking" multiple units together, extending their electronic structure and thereby modifying their properties.

mix_items.title: New African species confirms evolutionary origin of magic mushrooms
mix_items.summary: A long-standing debate about the evolutionary origin of the world's most widely cultivated "magic mushroom"—Psilocybe cubensis—may now have been settled by scientists from southern Africa and the United States.

mix_items.title: Scientists document Europe's first Jurassic lizard trackways in Asturias
mix_items.summary: The article presenting the research results was published online at the end of February in the journal Ichnos. The study focuses on two trackways (T1 and T2) preserved as convex hyporeliefs on the underside of a Late Jurassic sandstone layer, approximately 152 million years old, from the cliffs east of Playa de España (Villaviciosa). The specimens are now part of the museum's collection and are on display in the gallery dedicated to the Asturian Jurassic.

mix_items.title: Language difficulties can hinder young children's social autonomy
mix_items.summary: When considering the challenges faced by children with developmental language disorder (DLD), it's natural to think of difficulties they have in understanding and using language. What tends to get overlooked, however, is how the disorder impacts how their functioning in society.

mix_items.title: Middle East conflict exposes Africa’s fossil fuel risks & the case for clean energy
mix_items.summary: A deepening crisis in the Middle East could send economic shockwaves across sub-Saharan Africa, raising fuel costs, food prices and inflation across the region, according to a new analysis by energy consultancy Zero Carbon Analytics. Roughly one-fifth of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas flows through the Strait of Hormuz between Iran, Oman and […]

mix_items.title: Can Singapore rewild its lost reptiles?
mix_items.summary: In the older quarters of Singapore, fragments of forest persist like memories that refuse to fade. Within them live creatures that few residents ever see, and some that no longer exist there at all. The disappearance of species on an island is not always dramatic. Often it happens quietly, in decades of thinning habitat and […]

mix_items.title: Cambodian market survey a snapshot of a resilient — but stressed — Mekong
mix_items.summary: STUNG TRENG, Cambodia — Sunrise is still a long way off when the first fishing boats slip into the landing site at this provincial town along the Mekong River in northern Cambodia. The night’s catch is hauled ashore and moved in tubs and woven baskets to a nearby side street off the town’s main boulevard. […]

mix_items.title: Indigenous knowledge helps guide conservation of Australia’s endangered northern quoll
mix_items.summary: Despite its widespread biodiversity, Australia holds an unenviable record when it comes to wildlife: the highest mammalian extinction rate in the world. Since the arrival of Europeans and colonial expansion, at least 40 terrestrial mammal species have been lost, and others are facing serious threats. Notable among these is the northern quoll (Dasyurus hallucatus), a […]

mix_items.title: Ecuador’s new ecological corridor connects Andes and Amazon ecosystems
mix_items.summary: Ecuador has announced the creation of a new biological corridor designed to connect the eastern ranges of the Andes with the Amazon Rainforest, part of a larger initiative to strengthen ecological connectivity and protect biodiversity. The Llanganates–Yasuní Connectivity Corridor, officially announced this month, spans 2,159 square kilometers (834 square miles) across two provinces, connecting Llanganates […]

mix_items.title: In Malawi, farmers rebuild soil and livelihoods through agroecology
mix_items.summary: MZIMBA, Malawi – For years, life was defined by hardship for Grena Banda and her husband, Daniel Mwafulirwa, in Malawi’s northern district of Rumphi. Their small farm was their only reliable source of livelihood, yet it rarely produced enough. Climate change brought erratic rainfall, sometimes drought and sometimes heavy downpours that washed away fragile topsoil. […]

mix_items.title: David Chivers, student of the singing apes
mix_items.summary: Field primatology expanded rapidly in the late 20th century as biologists began to study apes and monkeys where they lived rather than only in museums or laboratories. Southeast Asia’s rainforests became an important setting for that shift. Among the researchers who helped shape the discipline there was David Chivers, a British primatologist whose work […]

mix_items.title: Critically endangered kākāpō parrot has standout breeding season
mix_items.summary: A total of 59 healthy kākāpō chicks have hatched over the last few weeks, according to the latest tally by Aotearoa New Zealand’s Department of Conservation. This marks one of the most successful recent breeding seasons for this critically endangered bird, whose last breeding season was four years ago. The kākāpō (Strigops habroptilus), a flightless bird […]

mix_items.title: Ugandans affected by pipeline discontented over rehabilitation efforts: Report
mix_items.summary: People whose land was acquired by the Ugandan government for the construction of the East African Crude Oil Pipeline (EACOP) say livelihood restoration programs offered by project developers have not changed their lives for the better, a new report says. The 1,443-kilometer (897-mile) crude oil pipeline — the longest heated crude oil pipeline in the […]

mix_items.title: Thailand tightens embrace of fossil fuels amid Middle East conflict
mix_items.summary: On March 4, Thailand’s government ordered the Ministry of Energy to secure new energy sources within a week to reduce the nation’s reliance on Middle Eastern oil. The directive follows the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, between the Persian Gulf and the Arabian Sea, after the Feb. 28 bombing of Iran by the U.S. […]

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Precision conservation: the rise of place-specific strategies where protection works best
mix_items.summary: Conservation has long wrestled with a deceptively simple question: not whether to act, but where action will matter most. Forest restoration, protected areas, wildlife corridors, and enforcement patrols all compete for limited funding across landscapes that differ enormously in ecology, governance, and human pressures. A growing body of research argues that improving outcomes depends less […]

mix_items.title: Local conservation projects restore habitats for wildlife
mix_items.summary: Community-led restoration efforts are improving biodiversity in several regions.

mix_items.title: Restoration of Brazil’s Atlantic Forest may hinge on market for native plants
mix_items.summary: The forests of the world are teeming with life, from the towering trees down to the microscopic organisms that quietly recycle and refuel the soil. That’s why clearing forests leads to biodiversity loss, problems with water and soil quality, and less carbon storage. In Brazil’s Atlantic Forest, restoration projects have been underway for decades to […]

mix_items.title: Indonesian communities push to reclaim lands following company permit revocations
mix_items.summary: MEDAN, Indonesia — Sahala Pasaribu, 35, walks on customary land his family has not been able to manage for more than three decades since PT Toba Pulp Lestari (TPL), a pulpwood company, took control of it. Now, after the government decided to revoke the company’s permit in January, he hopes it’s possible. “We feel free […]

joke_ro.text: - Azi am fost productiv: am mutat haosul dintr-un colt in altul.

satire_ro.title: CSAT: Grătarele vor fi interzise în acest weekend, ca să vadă avioanele americane pe unde merg
